plain_question: Will AI make life better in Canada?
pillars: Powering AI adoption; Empowering Canadians; Scaling Canadian champions
perspective_summary: The federal launch frames AI for All as a national prosperity, jobs, health care, adoption, and sovereignty strategy, with targets around GDP growth, jobs, youth placements, and business adoption.
app_use: Homepage overview; Future Canadian opening card
next_step: Turn targets into simple cards with receipts and dates.

plain_question: What does the strategy actually cover?
perspective_summary: The official strategy organizes the policy through six pillars: protection, empowerment, adoption, sovereign foundation, champions, and partnerships.
next_step: Create exhaustive pillar-to-question map.

plain_question: Can I trust it?
perspective_summary: Commentators accept trust as central but argue trust needs operational machinery: audits, appeal paths, incident reporting, enforcement, public registries, procurement rules, and consequences.
app_use: Concerned Canadian theme: Can I trust it?
next_step: Split into subquestions for audit, redress, disclosure, and enforcement.

plain_question: Will AI keep me and my daughter safe from deepfakes and harmful chatbots?
perspective_summary: The strategy promises future privacy and online safety laws, but critics say child-specific risk assessments, AI companion rules, audits, and standards are not yet concrete.
next_step: Add as one of the clearest public-facing safety questions.

plain_question: Who gets to use my personal data?
perspective_summary: The strategy promises modernized privacy law, protection for children's data, and action on surveillance pricing, but the legal text and enforcement model are still pending.
next_step: Track future privacy legislation when tabled.

plain_question: Will AI create better jobs or replace the jobs people need?
primary_personas: Future Canadian; Student / Future Worker; Workplace AI Adopter
perspective_summary: The strategy highlights job creation and training, but coverage and opposition voices note the lack of clear job-loss estimates and worker protection details.
next_step: Add a 'promise vs missing number' treatment.

plain_question: What should I study?
perspective_summary: The strategy funds AI literacy, student access to trusted AI agents, educator supports, and youth work placements, but definitions of trusted AI and actual curriculum choices need more detail.
next_step: Map to practical learning pathways without overclaiming job certainty.

plain_question: Can it help my business?
perspective_summary: The strategy names SME barriers and points to BDC LIFT, the Regional AI Initiative, readiness tools, and adoption supports for small and medium-sized businesses.
next_step: Turn programs into 'what help exists' and 'what is still vague'.

plain_question: Who will help normal organizations use AI safely?
primary_personas: Small Business Owner; Workplace AI Adopter; Future Canadian
perspective_summary: BC + AI argues national policy will fail without trusted local delivery: cohorts, credentials, practical governance artifacts, workflow design, and regional adoption intermediaries.
next_step: Use as an explainer sidebar: strategy vs delivery layer.

plain_question: How does government AI affect services I rely on?
perspective_summary: The strategy says government will be an anchor adopter and buyer of trusted Canadian AI to improve public services, but transparency, registry, procurement, and appeal paths need details.
next_step: Add public-sector specific safeguards and open questions.

plain_question: Will AI improve health care without putting patients at risk?
perspective_summary: The first AI Missions Program is health-focused, promising better diagnostics, patient care, and system efficiency, with funding aimed at improving health outcomes.
next_step: Pair benefits with health data governance questions.

plain_question: Will AI help farms, mines, factories, energy, and transportation?
perspective_summary: The strategy identifies health, energy and natural resources, transportation, agriculture, and manufacturing and robotics as priority sectors, with examples in farming, mining, manufacturing, and logistics.
next_step: Use as prosperity section beyond office-work AI.

plain_question: How do I scale my innovation?
pillars: Scaling Canadian champions; Sovereign AI foundation
perspective_summary: The strategy promises growth capital, compute access, commercialization programs, IP support, procurement, and support for globally competitive Canadian AI firms.
next_step: Convert into founder-facing path: capital, compute, customer, IP.

plain_question: If government picks AI winners, who benefits?
perspective_summary: BetaKit reports a shift from government as startup supporter to possible stakeholder and anchor customer. Critics want detail on selection criteria, incentives, conflicts, and public upside.
app_use: AI Entrepreneur and Future Canadian: Who benefits?
next_step: Add governance questions around public risk and private upside.

plain_question: Will Canadian data and companies be controlled from somewhere else?
perspective_summary: The AP and federal strategy emphasize foreign platform dependency, cloud concentration, Canadian data exposure, and the need for domestic leverage.
next_step: Make this a plain-language sovereignty card.

plain_question: Sovereign for whom?
primary_personas: Future Canadian; Concerned Canadian; AI Entrepreneur
perspective_summary: Kris Krug and BC + AI argue sovereign AI should mean public-interest access, host-Nation governance, transparent power allocation, community benefits, and accountability, not just Canadian-sited data centres.
next_step: Use as the main accountability lens for compute.

plain_question: Can Canadian compute create jobs and keep data here?
perspective_summary: TELUS and Vancouver Tech Journal emphasize 60000+ GPUs, 150 MW by 2032, 98% renewable power, closed-loop cooling, heat recovery, lower water use, and large economic value.
next_step: Pair with skeptic row so the app is balanced.

plain_question: Who pays for the power, water, heat, land, and noise?
perspective_summary: CityNews, The Tyee, and BC Greens surface concerns about water, electricity, grid strain, heat, noise, local consent, watershed protection, and whether municipalities can regulate data centres properly.
app_use: Future Canadian and Concerned Canadian infrastructure detail
next_step: Create side-by-side 'claimed benefits / unanswered local questions'.

plain_question: Will it respect what I create?
perspective_summary: News Media Canada and Krug note that copyright is missing or underdeveloped, especially around journalism, artists, educators, Indigenous cultural material, consent, compensation, and provenance.
next_step: Decide whether creator returns as persona or appears as issue module.

plain_question: Who controls Indigenous data, culture, land, and local infrastructure decisions?
pillars: Empowering Canadians; Sovereign AI foundation; Trusted partnerships
perspective_summary: The strategy promises Indigenous-led AI initiatives, but local commentary asks for host-Nation governance and contract-level participation around data, compute, culture, and land-based infrastructure.
next_step: Do not reduce to symbolism; capture governance mechanisms.

plain_question: Did the public really shape this strategy?
perspective_summary: Blair Attard-Frost argues the consultation was rushed and methodologically opaque, including unclear LLM use in summarizing submissions and unresolved questions around data handling and validation.
next_step: Add 'how this was made' explainer if app expands.

plain_question: Will Canada have open AI options instead of only big vendors?
primary_personas: AI Entrepreneur; Small Business Owner; Future Canadian
perspective_summary: The strategy supports open-source AI as a way to reduce vendor lock-in, lower costs, improve local adaptation, and offer alternatives to foreign proprietary platforms. Mozilla welcomed the direction.
next_step: Clarify what concrete programs support open-source adoption.

plain_question: How does Canada compare with places that have hard AI rules?
perspective_summary: The EU AI Act is a binding risk-based framework with obligations for high-risk AI, transparency duties for chatbots and deepfakes, and GPAI copyright-related disclosure requirements.
next_step: Use for 'Canada: directional; EU: hard law' comparisons.

plain_question: What can Canada learn from practical AI governance playbooks?
primary_personas: Small Business Owner; Workplace AI Adopter; AI Entrepreneur
pillars: Empowering Canadians; Powering AI adoption; Protecting Canadians
perspective_summary: Singapore pairs national AI ambition with practical governance playbooks and implementation tools that help organizations operationalize responsible AI.
next_step: Use for practical adoption/governance design examples.
